text: robotic assisted repair of sciatic notch hernia. This is a seventy-one-year-old female who presented with symptoms of intermittent low right back pain associated with right lower quadrant pain, with a posterior right-sided visible and palpable defect. To this point, she was considered to have a paraspinal tumor being surveilled with MRI. However, she progressed to obstructive symptoms and on repeat MRI was found to have a bowel-containing right sciatic notch hernia.

The video shows a posterior view of the pelvis with a focus on the sciatic foramina, highlighting the potential exit points for a sciatic hernia through the greater or lesser sciatic foramen. An intraabdominal view reveals the internal iliac vessels near the sciatic foramen, along with the piriformis, external obturator, and levator ani muscles. A perioperative ureteral stent is visible, and the patient is positioned supine with a Foley catheter inserted.

Three eight-millimeter robotic ports are positioned in an oblique line toward the right pelvis for the right-sided unilateral hernia repair. A twelve-millimeter assistant port is placed laterally in the right lower quadrant. Upon gaining abdominal access, the ovary is identified and suspended away from the operative field, exposing the broad ligament as the entry point into the retroperitoneum. The ureter is identified, and proper stent placement is confirmed.

The surgeon parietalizes the peritoneum of the broad ligament to create the preperitoneal space, ensuring the correct plane is entered by confirming the integrity of the opposite side of the broad ligament.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the retroperitoneal vessels, including the internal iliac vein, with the sacrum visible as a fixation point for the mesh. The contour of the sciatic hernia is revealed, and the contents of the hernia sac are fully reduced.

The surgeon identifies the internal iliac vein and artery, with the sacrum fully dissected as the posteromedial point for mesh fixation. The fibers of the piriformis are visible, serving as the posterolateral fixation point. A hybrid mesh, consisting of a permanent layer and an absorbable layer, is deployed and trimmed to fit the dissection area. The absorbable layer is oriented medially to protect delicate retroperitoneal structures, including the sciatic nerve.

The surgeon interposes absorbable mesh between the ureter and permanent mesh to reduce the risk of ureteral erosion, adhesion, and obstruction. The mesh is sutured to the sacrum using IIO permanent suture and then fixed to the piriformis muscle. After securing the posterior edge, the mesh is trimmed to fit the dissection pocket.

The surgeon fixes the mesh to the external obturator muscle at the anterolateral point and to the levator ani muscles at the anteromedial point. The mesh is then surveyed to ensure proper placement. Finally, the peritoneal defect is closed using a running barbed absorbable suture.

Sciatic notch hernia is an exceedingly rare pathology with less than a hundred descriptions in the literature. There is no surgical standard for this repair. We employ a repair using a hybrid mesh to reduce the risk of mesh injuring vulnerable structures in this area, such as the sciatic nerve, iliac vessels, and particularly the ureter. Keen knowledge of the local anatomy will ensure a successful repair.
